_meta.title: Planting & Care, Per Plant — Alex's Yard, Larkmont (Arid Region, ~300 m)
_meta.purpose_he: Planting and care recommendations for each plant: soil, container vs. ground (and container size), fertilization (what and when), and special requirements. Grounded in data collected about Alex's site + cultivation knowledge for each species.
_meta.site_he: Soil group X1 / USDA Aridisols (Cambids) — shallow, calcareous, low in organic matter over bedrock, with eroded loess; fast drainage, low water-holding capacity. Annual evaporation ~1700–1800 mm; arid moisture regime (irrigation-dependent). Winter with real frosts + a very hot summer.
_meta.honesty_he: General guidance for the site's conditions — not a guarantee. Actual results depend on the variety, the rootstock, the plant's age, and the microclimate. Check with a nursery / local guide and with the fertilizer label.
_meta.curated_from: harvest/agriculture/ (agri-ministry GIS, FAO-56, pomology literature) + app/data/agro.json — 2026-06-09
general_soil_prep_he: A wide, deep planting pit (≥60×60×60 cm), breaking the bedrock horizon / calcareous hardpan at the bottom for root drainage.
Mix the local soil with mature compost (~⅓ of the volume) + tuff/coarse gravel for drainage.
Thick mulch around the trunk — reduces evaporation, cools the root, and adds organic matter.
Frequent, measured drip irrigation (shallow soil doesn't store water); seasonal leaching to wash out salt.

sources.name_he: Ministry of Agriculture — soil/evaporation GIS layers (regional, USDA, Newhall)
Agricultural research — breaking dormancy under deficient winter chill
Royal Horticultural Society — growing guides for fruit trees and shrubs
Univ. of California Agriculture & Natural Resources — nuts/stone fruit/citrus
California Rare Fruit Growers — fig, pomegranate, loquat, jujube, avocado

tips.soil_he: The odd one out in the yard: requires acidic soil (pH 4.5–5.5) — the complete opposite of the plateau's basic, calcareous ground. Don't plant in the local soil. A special mix: peat / coconut coir + pine needles + perlite; irrigate only with soft water (rain/reverse osmosis) — calcareous tap water raises the pH.
tips.container_he: In a container only (or an isolated bed), 30–50 L for a mature shrub — this way you control the acidity and keep the roots away from the lime. An east-facing position (morning sun, afternoon shade) to reduce heat stress.
tips.fertilizer_he: An 'acid-loving' fertilizer (nitrogenous, such as ammonium sulfate / azalea fertilizer) in small doses in spring and early summer. No lime and no fresh manure. Elemental sulfur to maintain the pH.
tips.special_he: High chill requirement (~800 hours) — Larkmont may provide it. Sensitive to extreme heat and drought; steady drip is essential. Two varieties are worthwhile for cross-pollination and a larger yield.

tips.soil_he: Tolerates the shallow calcareous soil but requires excellent drainage — stone fruits are sensitive to 'wet feet' and root-collar rot. A deep pit that breaks the bedrock, mature compost + tuff. Plant on a slight mound to drain the collar.
tips.container_he: In the ground (a mature fruit tree). In a container only for a 'dwarf' variety — at least 40–50 L — but the ground is preferable, in the back yard with a free canopy.
tips.fertilizer_he: Mature compost in autumn around the root ring. Balanced NPK (or a fruit-tree fertilizer) in spring at bud break; stop nitrogen at the end of summer so the tree hardens off toward winter.
tips.special_he: Blooms early (February–March) — late spring frosts can wipe out an entire crop; cover with fabric on cold nights. Light pruning after harvest (not in the wet winter — to reduce infection). Most varieties are self-pollinating.

tips.soil_he: Ideally suited to the site: tolerates lime, salt, and shallow soil almost better than any other tree. Still — a wide pit + compost improves fruit set. Drainage matters; less sensitive than most trees.
tips.container_he: In the ground or in a container — both are good. In a container: 40–60 L for a fruit-bearing tree; on the patio it will get most of the sun and set fruit abundantly.
tips.fertilizer_he: A light feeder. Mature compost in autumn; a little balanced NPK in spring. Excess nitrogen = lots of leaves, little fruit — avoid it. Potassium helps fruit quality.
tips.special_he: Low chill requirement (~150 hrs) + a long, hot summer = a classic arid-zone crop. Irregular irrigation during ripening causes the fruit to split — keep moisture uniform. Minimal pruning; remove suckers from the base.

tips.soil_he: An easily adaptable Mediterranean tree — tolerates the shallow calcareous soil well. A wide pit, compost + good drainage. In fact, soil that isn't too rich + controlled watering produces sweeter fruit.
tips.container_he: In the ground for a large, shady tree; in a container the restricted roots encourage fruit — 40–60 L, and easy to move away from frost. Excellent drainage is a must; it likes slight dryness between waterings.
tips.fertilizer_he: Low-need. Mature compost in autumn; if growth is weak — a little balanced fertilizer in spring. Excess nitrogen delays fruit and promotes foliage — avoid it.
tips.special_he: Very low chill requirement; two crops are possible (breba + main). Prune in winter during dormancy. Key pest: the fig fly (Silba adipata) — organic option: neem oil. Most local varieties are parthenocarpic (no need for the blastophaga wasp).

tips.soil_he: The most demanding plant in the yard. Requires perfect drainage — very sensitive to 'wet feet' and to Phytophthora. Also sensitive to salt and lime (chlorosis in calcareous soil). Plant on a raised mound, an organic mix + tuff; a salt/lime-tolerant rootstock is essential.
tips.container_he: A large container (≥60–100 L) is preferable at the site — you control drainage and acidity, and it's easy to protect from frost. In the ground only in a wind-sheltered spot on a well-drained slope.
tips.fertilizer_he: Compost in autumn; a citrus/avocado fertilizer in small, frequent doses (spring–summer). Iron/zinc injections against calcareous chlorosis. Sensitive to excess salt — periodic leaching.
tips.special_he: Sensitive to cold (damage from 0° to -2°C, depending on the variety) — in Larkmont's dry cold it needs a sheltered, raised spot and cover on frosty nights; prefer a hardy variety (Mexican). High water need — steady drip in summer. A/B types for pollination.

tips.soil_he: Soil-tolerant — likes moist soil rich in organic matter. A regular garden mix + compost. Holds moisture better than most of the site's plants.
tips.container_he: A container is recommended (10–15 L) — it spreads aggressively in the ground and crowds out other plants; the container keeps that in check. A west-facing/partial-shade position prevents leaf scorch at midday.
tips.fertilizer_he: Compost or a balanced leaf fertilizer (nitrogen preferred) every few weeks during the growing season for fragrant, fresh foliage.
tips.special_he: Needs frequent cutting back (encourages fresh growth and prevents flowering). Loves water — don't let it dry out. In a cold winter it may die back and return in spring. Needs no chill or pollination.

tips.soil_he: The classic plateau tree — tolerates shallow, calcareous soil, but requires good drainage (sensitive to root rot). A deep pit that breaks the bedrock + compost. A lime-tolerant rootstock (such as GF-677) is helpful.
tips.container_he: In the ground (a landscape tree). In the back yard with full sun. A container only for a dwarf variety, and not recommended long-term.
tips.fertilizer_he: Compost in autumn; balanced NPK + potassium in spring. Once established, a low need. Zinc is important for almonds (a common deficiency in calcareous soil).
tips.special_he: Blooms the earliest (as early as February) — high risk of spring frosts at altitude; prefer a late-blooming variety. Most varieties need a pollinator (a second variety + bees); some are self-pollinating (Independence). Medium chill requirement — pick a low-chill variety.

tips.soil_he: A tough Mediterranean tree that actually likes calcareous, shallow, poor soil — a perfect fit for the site. Good drainage; a little compost at planting is enough. Tolerates salt and drought.
tips.container_he: In the ground (a large tree with pleasant shade). A container only while young; deep-rooted — move it to the ground early.
tips.fertilizer_he: Almost no need. Mature compost in autumn during the first years; afterward it gets by on its own. Excess fertilizer is unnecessary and even harmful.
tips.special_he: Almost no irrigation after establishment (2–3 years). Dioecious in most cases — needs a male tree + a female for a crop (or a hermaphrodite variety). Cold-tolerant only down to -7° — watch out for severe frosts.

tips.soil_he: A symbol of Mediterranean dryness — thrives in calcareous, shallow, poor soil. Good drainage matters (it tolerates salt, not standing moisture). A pit + moderate compost; don't over-enrich the soil.
tips.container_he: In the ground (a long-lived tree). A container is possible for a small tree — 40–60 L — but the ground is preferable, in the back yard.
tips.fertilizer_he: A light feeder. Compost in autumn; moderate nitrogen in spring before flowering. Excess water and fertilizer harms the oil quality.
tips.special_he: Needs a little winter chill to induce flowering — the dry cold helps. Most varieties are self-pollinating, but a second pollinator variety raises the yield. Prune to open the canopy to light and air. Pest: the olive fly.

tips.soil_he: "Feet in water, head in fire" — the most salt- and lime-tolerant tree; almost any soil will do if there's drainage and water at depth. A wide pit + sand/tuff.
tips.container_he: In the ground (a giant tree, deep-rooted). Not suited to a container long-term — only for temporary growing in the first years.
tips.fertilizer_he: A medium feeder. A fertilizer rich in nitrogen + potassium + magnesium (a palm fertilizer) in spring and summer; potassium/magnesium deficiency is common in palms.
tips.special_he: No chill requirement, but it needs extreme heat to ripen — Larkmont's cool plateau may delay ripening (a hotter, lower area is preferable). Dioecious — needs male pollen or hand pollination.

tips.soil_he: Well suited to calcareous, shallow, well-drained soil — in fact poor soil yields quality grapes. A pit + a little compost; no standing moisture.
tips.container_he: In the ground for an arbor/pergola (green summer shade + grapes). A container is possible, 30–50 L with support. On the patio it will get full sun.
tips.fertilizer_he: A light feeder. Compost in autumn; a little NPK + potassium in spring. Excess nitrogen = lots of foliage, little fruit.
tips.special_he: Winter pruning is essential for a crop (cut back to two buds / canes). Low chill requirement; in a mild winter, Dormex is used to even out bud break. Pest: the grape berry moth; powdery mildew — sulfur. Most varieties are self-pollinating.

tips.soil_he: Any well-drained soil works — it actually prefers poor sandy/stony soil. Root rot is the only danger: perfect drainage is a must, with no heavy organic matter.
tips.container_he: In the ground or a container (20–40 L, a cactus mix). Excellent as a living hedge. Plant a pad (cladode) that has been dried for a few days to callus the cut.
tips.fertilizer_he: Almost none. A low-nitrogen cactus fertilizer once in spring, if at all. Excess water and fertilizer = rot.
tips.special_he: The lowest water need in the yard — almost no irrigation. Watch out for the tiny spines (glochids). Flowers and bears fruit (sabras) in summer; needs no chill or cross-pollination.

tips.soil_he: A Mediterranean herb shrub that likes calcareous, shallow, well-drained soil — perfect for the site. Hates standing moisture; sand/tuff for drainage.
tips.container_he: In a container (10–20 L) or in the ground. Excellent for the patio in full sun; attracts bees.
tips.fertilizer_he: Almost none. Light compost in spring is enough. Excess fertilizer and water weakens the aroma and promotes rot.
tips.special_he: Almost maintenance-free; light pruning to shape it and encourage fresh growth. Tolerates drought and mild cold. Needs no chill or pollination.

tips.soil_he: Likes calcareous (alkaline), poor, very well-drained soil — the site suits it. Drainage is critical: root rot is the main cause of death. Sand/gravel in the mix.
tips.container_he: In a container (15–25 L) or in well-drained ground. Suits the patio in full sun; attracts pollinators.
tips.fertilizer_he: Almost none — it likes lean soil. A little compost in spring at most. Excess nitrogen = lots of leaves and few fragrant flowers.
tips.special_he: Light pruning after flowering to keep its shape (don't cut back into old wood). Tolerates drought and cold. Needs no chill or cross-pollination.

tips.soil_he: A tough Mediterranean culinary herb — likes calcareous, shallow, well-drained soil. Hates wet feet. Good drainage is enough.
tips.container_he: In a container (10–20 L) or in the ground — both are good. Also suits the west-facing front with afternoon sun.
tips.fertilizer_he: Almost none. Light compost in spring. A low feeder — don't overdo it.
tips.special_he: Light pruning in spring to encourage fresh growth; replace it after 3–4 years when it turns woody. Tolerates cold and drought; almost maintenance-free. Needs no chill or pollination.

tips.soil_he: A true arid-zone tree that tolerates calcareous, saline, and shallow soil excellently — very well suited to the site. Good drainage is a must (sensitive to Verticillium / root rot). A tolerant rootstock (P. atlantica / UCB-1).
tips.container_he: In the ground (a landscape tree, deep-rooted). Not suited to a container long-term.
tips.fertilizer_he: Compost in autumn; nitrogen + potassium + zinc in spring. A medium feeder; zinc is important in calcareous soil.
tips.special_he: High chill requirement (~600 hrs) — the plateau's dry cold suits it if the variety gets enough. Dioecious — needs one male tree per ~8 females for pollination (by wind). Bears fruit in alternation (an on/off year).

tips.soil_he: A low, hardy culinary plant — likes calcareous, stony, poor, well-drained soil. Wonderful as a ground cover between stones. Hates moisture.
tips.container_he: In a container (5–15 L) or in the ground / between the front-yard stones. Suits the west-facing front.
tips.fertilizer_he: Almost none. A little compost in spring at most. Likes lean soil — excess fertilizer weakens the aroma.
tips.special_he: Light pruning after flowering to keep its shape. Tolerates drought and cold; almost maintenance-free. Attracts pollinators. Needs no chill or pollination.

tips.soil_he: Tolerates a range of soils including calcareous, but requires good drainage. A wide pit + compost. An evergreen tree — more tolerant than stone fruits but less than fig/pomegranate in saline soil.
tips.container_he: In the ground or a large container (≥40–60 L). On the raised patio it will be better protected from ground frosts.
tips.fertilizer_he: Compost in autumn; balanced NPK in spring and after harvest. A medium feeder.
tips.special_he: Blooms in autumn/winter and bears fruit in early spring — winter frosts can damage the flowers/young fruit; a sheltered spot matters in the dry cold. Most varieties are self-pollinating. Thinning the fruit improves size.

tips.soil_he: An especially tough fruit tree that tolerates calcareous, saline, alkaline, and shallow soil — one of the easiest for the site. Good drainage is enough; almost no requirements.
tips.container_he: In the ground (a small landscape tree). A container is possible (≥40 L) but the ground is preferable.
tips.fertilizer_he: A very low feeder. Compost in autumn during the first years is enough; afterward almost no fertilizer.
tips.special_he: Tolerates extreme heat, drought, and also cold (down to -15°). Bears fruit even without care. Sends up suckers from the root — remove them. Most varieties are self-pollinating, but a second variety raises the yield.
